coredata.dc:title: PAM triggered visual “green-yellow-orange” ratiometric fluorescence probes in CRISPR/Cas12a: Universal detection of exosome and APE1
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Zhigang
Chen, Xiang
Yu, Yan
Xu, Guanhong
Wei, Fangdi
Yang, Jing
Hu, Qin
Cen, Yao
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) probes in CRISPR/Cas12a system are typically incorporated at the signal generation phase. Nonetheless the probes need costly labeling and labor-intensive purification of ssDNA. The single channel transmission is subject to interference from the environment and the instrument, which results in fluctuations that are detrimental to accurate quantitative analysis. Therefore, it is incredibly critical to engineer a novel double-emission fluorescence indicator in CRISPR/Cas12a system. Here, protospacer-adjacent motif (PAM) sequence was extended at the 3′ termini of single-strand activator to construct a hairpin activator for enhancing its capability to locate CRISPR/Cas12a. The activated Cas12a could accelerate T40 (ssDNA containing 40 repeat T bases) digestion, which disrupted its interaction with A40 (ssDNA containing 40 repeat A bases) and Gelred, unable to produce red fluorescence. Lastly, the carbon dots emitting green fluorescence (g-CDs) were utilized as built-in correction. Therefore, a visual ratiometric fluorescence probe dubbed “g-CDs/Gelred” was constructed, and the fluorescence color switch of “green-yellow-orange” could be observed upon the alteration of activator concentration. The g-CDs/Gelred probe has the features of label-free, visualization, universality, and strong anti-interference ability. By modifying the hairpin activator with cholesterol or AP site, the g-CDs/Gelred probe was adapted to quantitatively detect exosome and APE1 in actual biological sample, thus confirming the versatility of this sensing platform. Our research extends the application scope of traditional nanomaterials and enriches the CRISPR/Cas12a biosensor ecosystem through offering a visual signal output platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CRISPR/Cas12a
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Visual output
Label-free
Universal detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Diverse DNA sequences bind to Gelred
Construction of the g-CDs/Gelred ratiometric fluorescence probe
Principle of g-CDs/Gelred probe for detecting exosome and APE1
Performance of Gelred in CRISPR/Cas12a system
Construction and visualization of g-CDs/Gelred probe in CRISPR/Cas12a
Analysis performance of the constructed system for exosome
Analysis performance of the constructed system for APE1

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81973283, 61775099, 21705080), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Nos. BK20221304, BK20171487, BK20171043), \u201CBlue Project\u201D Foundation of the Higher Education Institutions of Jiangsu Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence intensity when diverse sequences of DNA were intermixed with Gelred. Fluorescence intensity (B) and signal-to-background ratio (C) of the Cas12a system by cleaving different ssDNA substrates. (D) Schematic diagram of three activators (20nt, HP1 and HP2) triggering Cas12a to degrade T40. (E) Comparison of fluorescence trends of the three activators activating Cas12a to destroy T40 at varying activator concentration (0, 1, 2, 3, 6 nM). Inset: A photograph of the fluorescence illuminated by a UV lamp. (F) Comparison of fluorescence signal-to-background ratio of three activators. (G) Agarose gel electrophoresis for investigating the cleavage samples of T40-A40, and each activator concentration was 2.5 nM. (H) Agarose gel electrophoresis analysis of T40 degradation products after Cas12a activated by the three activators.

(A) Optimization of the poly-T chain length. (B) Fluorescence signal-to-background ratio of different poly-T substrates. (C) Optimization of the Cas12a/crRNA binary complex concentration. (D) Optimization of the Cas12a incubation time. (E) Fluorescence curves of T40-A40 at differing HP2 concentrations. Inset: A photograph of fluorescence color shifting under an ultraviolet lamp. (F) The trend of fluorescence response with rising HP2 concentration.

Diagram of T40-A40 fluorescent response with g-CDs when the activator was present (A) or absent (B). (C) Fluorescence curves of the g-CDs/Gelred in response to varying activator concentrations. Inset: A photograph of fluorescence color alterations under an ultraviolet lamp. (D) The trend of the ratiometric fluorescence response of g-CDs/Gelred with increasing activator concentration.

(A) Schematic diagram of the fluorescence feasibility evaluation of Chol-2 (cholesterol modified at the 5′ termini of HP2) anchoring on exosome surfaces. (B) Comparison of fluorescence curves in exosome lysates when Chol-2 was present or absent. (C) Fluorescence curves of the g-CDs/Gelred in response to variable exosome concentrations. Inset: photograph of fluorescence chromaticity shifting under ultraviolet light. (D) The response trend of g-CDs/Gelred during the elevation in exosome concentration. Inset: the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and exosome concentrations within the range of 7.5 × 106-4.5 × 107 particles/mL.

(A) Fluorescence curves of the g-CDs/Gelred ratiometric system in response to varying concentrations of APE1. Inset: A photograph of fluorescence color alteration under an ultraviolet lamp. (B) APE1 concentration versus ratiometric fluorescent value. Inset: the linear relationship between F605/F520 and APE1 concentrations within the range of 0.05–0.25 U/mL.

(A) The fluorescent responses towards various concentrations of APE1 in MCF-7 cell lysates (0, 350, 400, 750, 1600, 2100, 2200, 2600 Cells/mL). (B) Fluorescence trend with increasing concentrations of APE1 in MCF-7 cell lysates. Inset: Graph of fluorescence color shift under an ultraviolet lamp (top), and the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio and concentrations of APE1 within the range of 350–2200 Cells/mL (bottom). (C) The fluorescence intensity ratio (F605/F520) of APE1 from different cancer cell lysates (5000 Cells/mL).

The working principle of the g-CDs/Gelred ratiometric probe to detect exosome (A) and APE1 (B). (C) Fluorescence trends change of the g-CDs/Gelred probe with increasing target concentration and the visualization effect under UV light.
Exosomes were determined in 9 μL supernatant of starvation cultured MCF-7 cells (n = 5).

head.ce:title: PAM triggered visual “green-yellow-orange” ratiometric fluorescence probes in CRISPR/Cas12a: Universal detection of exosome and APE1

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated Taizhou People’s Hospital of Nanjing Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Northern Jiangsu Institute of Clinical Medicine, The Affiliated Huaian No.1 People’s Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Huaian, Jiangsu 223300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Northern Jiangsu Institute of Clinical Medicine
The Affiliated Huaian No.1 People’s Hospital of Nanjing Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Northern Jiangsu Institute of Clinical Medicine, The Affiliated Huaian No.1 People’s Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Huaian, Jiangsu 223300, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, China (Yao Cen).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) probes in CRISPR/Cas12a system are typically incorporated at the signal generation phase. Nonetheless the probes need costly labeling and labor-intensive purification of ssDNA. The single channel transmission is subject to interference from the environment and the instrument, which results in fluctuations that are detrimental to accurate quantitative analysis. Therefore, it is incredibly critical to engineer a novel double-emission fluorescence indicator in CRISPR/Cas12a system. Here, protospacer-adjacent motif (PAM) sequence was extended at the 3′ termini of single-strand activator to construct a hairpin activator for enhancing its capability to locate CRISPR/Cas12a. The activated Cas12a could accelerate T40 (ssDNA containing 40 repeat T bases) digestion, which disrupted its interaction with A40 (ssDNA containing 40 repeat A bases) and Gelred, unable to produce red fluorescence. Lastly, the carbon dots emitting green fluorescence (g-CDs) were utilized as built-in correction. Therefore, a visual ratiometric fluorescence probe dubbed “g-CDs/Gelred” was constructed, and the fluorescence color switch of “green-yellow-orange” could be observed upon the alteration of activator concentration. The g-CDs/Gelred probe has the features of label-free, visualization, universality, and strong anti-interference ability. By modifying the hairpin activator with cholesterol or AP site, the g-CDs/Gelred probe was adapted to quantitatively detect exosome and APE1 in actual biological sample, thus confirming the versatility of this sensing platform. Our research extends the application scope of traditional nanomaterials and enriches the CRISPR/Cas12a biosensor ecosystem through offering a visual signal output platform.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) and CRISPR-associated proteins (Cas) are an adaptive immune system which enables bacteria and archaea to resist invasion by bacteriophages or plasmids [1]. Recent years have witnessed extensive exploration and application of genetic editing technologies involving CRISPR/Cas [2,3]. Meanwhile, the CRISPR/Cas technique also presents significant exploitation potential in bioimaging [4,5] and biosensing [6,7]. In various classes of Cas proteins, both Cas12a substrate and target are deoxyribonucleic acid chains. As well as being competent to identify both ssDNA and dsDNA, CRISPR/Cas12a-based sensors possess an exceptional degree of flexibility. The Cas12a protein will conduct its crRNA-directed cis-cleavage and trans-cleavage activities once recognizing the T-rich protospacer-adjacent motif (PAM) sequence on dsDNA, and is frequently utilized as a signal amplification agent to distinguish and determine multiple nucleic acid molecules [8,9].

By integrating distinct properties of nanomaterials and signal transducers downstream of CRISPR/Cas12a, a variety of signal output modes are accessible. Current researches focusing on Cas12a-based fluorescence analysis [10], colorimetry [11], and electrochemical approaches [12]. Contrary to other analytical techniques, fluorescence spectroscopy is notably desirable on account of its simplicity, affordable operating charges, prompt response, remarkable sensitivity, and superior selectivity. A wide range of single-emission fluorescence assays have been devised recently via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) probes in CRISPR/Cas12a systems [13,14]. Nevertheless, the FRET probes designed for Cas12a necessitate tagging both termini of ssDNA with fluorophores and quenchers (F-Q probes). Following modification, purification is imperative to remove excess fluorophores and quenchers, leading to additional complexity and expenditure [15,16]. Moreover, probes that rely on fluorescence intensity at single wavelengths are adversely affected by probe concentrations, test environments and experimental operation. Fluorescence sensors with single emission are appropriate for the sensitive identification of trace substances in biological samples, but the single emission fluorescence probe may be photobleached under high intensity laser irradiation, which makes the output signal susceptible to interference from the external environment [17].

Dual-emission fluorescence probes, also known as ratiometric fluorescence probes, rely on the ratio of fluorescence emission intensity at two different emission wavelengths to analyze the concentration of the target, which means that the probe's response is not affected by the single emission intensity. Dual-emission fluorescence probes typically offer built-in calibration that eliminates interference generated by laser intensity, background signals and experimental operation error, thus improving the accuracy of analytical methods in comparison to single-emission fluorescence probes [18,19]. Additionally, by observing the relative change of the two fluorescence emission intensities, the ratiometric sensor can achieve excellent visual distinction effect with the naked eyes [20]. However, only a few publications have reported the integration of ratio-type fluorescence probes in CRISPR systems. For instance, Yang et al. constructed a ratiometric fluorescence probe for Pb2+ determination through coupling CDs@ZIF-8 with FAM-tagged ssDNA [21]. Ma et al. incorporated CRISPR/Cas12a technology and DNA templated silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) to realize ultra-sensitive ratiometric assays of Salmonella Typhus [22]. And Hong et al. screened out the conformational variable AgNCs synthesis template and integrated it into the Cas12a platform to generate a label-free biosensor [23]. However, these probes required costly fluorophore modification or complex preparation processes to obtain corresponding fluorescent materials. Moreover, their fluorescence brightness was relatively low, rendering them inadequate for visual differentiation. Therefore, the development of ratiometric fluorescence probes in CRISPR/Cas12a system with label-free processes, facile operation procedures, richer fluorescence color alterations and stronger visual distinction effects still indicates a broad demand window.

To establish a non-labeled and visible ratiometric probe in CRISPR/Cas12a, we initially extended the full-length ssDNA activator (20nt) to obtain a hairpin activator HP2, which comprised the PAM sequence. Since PAM sequences could bind to PAM-interacting (PI) domain of Cas12a, the hairpin activator possessed enhanced targeting and activation abilities against Cas12a. Following the cleavage of ssDNA T40 by activated Cas12a, the red-emitting fluorescent dye (Gelred) and green-emitting carbon dots (g-CDs) were added to the CRISPR/Cas12a system for constructing a visual ratiometric fluorescence biosensor. Subsequently, the g-CDs/Gelred probe was employed for quantitative analysis of exosome. As cholesterol could be incorporated into phospholipid bilayers within exosome, we removed the MNPs/exosome/activator complex by magnetic separation. Then we added Cas12a to the supernatant for the subsequent assay. With the exosome concentration increased, a transition in fluorescence color from green to yellow to orange could be observed with the naked eye under UV lamps, which enabled visual assessment. In order to verify the universality of the proposed ratiometric sensor, the AP site was modified on the activator, and APE1 could be quantitatively analyzed because APE1 could cut the AP site on the activator.
Reagents and instruments as well as DNA sequences are provided in Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diverse DNA sequences bind to Gelred
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the affinity between Gelred and ssDNA or dsDNA, various sequences of ssDNA or dsDNA (1.4 μM) were introduced to the centrifuge tube at room temperature along with Gelred (1800 times diluted), maintaining a total system volume at 100 μL, and fluorescence detection was conducted after the vortex. The reagent concentrations were all given at their final concentration in the corresponding volumes. Finally, a fluorescence intensity measurement at 605 nm was completed with the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, and the slit widths were 5 nm and 10 nm, respectively. The voltage was set as 800 V.

To explore the activation properties of varying activation chains on CRISPR/Cas12a, the activators with three different sequences (20nt, HP1 and HP2), Cas12a/crRNA binary complex (25 nM), NEBuffer 2.1 (50 mm NaCl, 10 mM Tris-HCl, 10 mM MgCl2, 100 μg/ml BSA, pH 7.9@25 °C) and substrate T40 (1.4 μM) were contained in 80 μL solution, and the experimental process continued for 100 min at 37 °C. Afterward, A40 (1.12 μM), Gelred (1800 times diluted) and ultrapure water were added to the system, and the final system volume was 100 μL. Then, the fluorescence of the system was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the g-CDs/Gelred ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: To construct a visual ratiometric fluorescence probe, the g-CDs were prepared through a hydrothermal method (Supplementary material). Once the substrate chain T40 reacted with Cas12a, the A40, Gelred, and g-CDs were added to generate signal output. With the addition of g-CDs to the above system, the excitation wavelength was selected at 370 nm and the slit width was set at 5 and 10 nm. Then the fluorescence intensities at 520 and 605 nm were recorded.

Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 MNPs) modified with CD63 aptamer were utilized to capture exosomes. First, we synthesized MNPs with a carboxyl-rich surface (MNPs-COOH). Then, the EDC/NHS process was implemented to immobilize the aminated CD63 aptamer on MNPs-COOH, and the resulting MNPs-CD63 apt was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments. Detailed research procedures, including the instructions for cultivating MCF-7 cells and preparing exosome samples, were described in the Supplementary materials.

To establish a standard curve for exosome, different concentrations of exosomes were mixed with MNPs-CD63 apt (0.25 mg/mL) and incubated at 25 °C for 60 min. The supernatant was discarded by magnetic separation to obtain the MNPs-CD63 apt/exosome complex. Then, Chol-2 (3.5 nM) was transferred to the centrifuge tube, and the volume was enlarged to 50 μL using 1 × PBS. Having performed the reaction at 25 °C for 60 min, 40 μL of the supernatant was magnetically separated and transferred to a new centrifuge tube. Next, Cas12a/crRNA binary complex (25 nM), NEBuffer 2.1 and substrate T40 (1.4 μM) were introduced into the centrifuge tube and reacted at 37 °C for 100 min. Finally, A40 (1.12 μM) and Gelred (1800 times diluted) were applied to the above system, and b-CDs were incorporated to make the ultimate volume to100 μL. Following the vortex was uniform, fluorescence determination could be conducted.

To investigate the suitability of this strategy to exosome analysis in actual samples, the MCF-7 cell starvation culture supernatant (pre-clearance of cells and cell debris) was transferred to the mixed solution of MNPs-CD63 apt (0.25 mg/mL) with low, medium and high concentrations of exosome extracts. The subsequent operation process was in accordance with the previous experimental steps.

Initially, varied concentrations of APE1 were incubated with HP2-AP (3.5 nM) and NEBuffer 4 (50 mM potassium acetate, 20 mM Tris-acetate, 10 mM magnesium acetate, 1 mM dithiothreitol, pH 7.9@25 °C), then the blend was supplemented to 30 μL with ultra-pure water, and reacted at 37 °C for 60 min. Afterwards, Cas12a/crRNA binary complex (25 nM), 1 × NEBuffer 2.1 and substrate T40 (1.4 μM) were introduced. The obtained 80 μL solution was incubated at 37 °C for 100 min. Following the addition of A40 (1.12 μM) and Gelred (1800 times diluted), the volume was adjusted to 100 μL by g-CDs and ultra-pure water. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F605/F520) was calculated as the signal output outcome.

To determine APE1 activity in actual samples, differing volumes of MCF-7 cell lysates were blended into a mixture of HP2-AP (3.5 nM) and NEBuffer 4. The subsequent operation procedure was consistent with the aforementioned experimental steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of g-CDs/Gelred probe for detecting exosome and APE1
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the quantitative analysis ability of the biosensor built for biomarkers, we utilized the g-CDs/Gelred probe to detect exosomes and APE1. Exosomes are extracellular vesicles (30–150 nm in diameter) secreted by cells [24,25], whose membrane protein types and expression levels are directly in relation to cancer occurrence and progression [26–28]. For conventional exosome analysis strategies, a large sample volume, sophisticated instruments, and time-consuming pretreatment operations are generally required [29–31]. In order to facilitate the exosome extraction process and shorten analysis time, magnetic separation technology is adopted in most studies [32]. Moreover, signal amplification tools such as CRISPR/Cas12a have also been employed for exosome analysis to improve sensitivity while reducing the required sample volume [33]. However, the current researches show that most of reports on exosome determination utilizing magnetic separation and CRISPR/Cas12a technology are single-emission fluorescence sensors based on expensive F-Q probes [34,35]. Consequently, we combined the magnetic separation method with g-CDs/Gelred ratiometric probe to achieve exosome assay.

Scheme 1 A illustrated the principle of magnetic separation methods combined with CRISPR/Cas12a for capturing and detecting exosomes. When the exosome was present, the CD63 protein at the surface of exosomes would be captured by MNPs modified with CD63 apt, thereby obtaining the MNPs/exosome complex. After magnetic separation, the cholesterol modified HP2 added was anchored to exosomes by inserting its end-modified cholesterol into their phospholipid bilayer. Upon magnetic separation, Cas12a/crRNA and T40 was added to the supernatant, Cas12a could not be activated, and substrate chain T40 remained intact. Finally, the addition of complementary chain A40 allowed to form stable A40/T40 dsDNA. Gelred inserted into A40/T40 and emitted bright red fluorescence. The red fluorescence changed into orange after mixed with g-CDs. In the absence of exosomes, only MNPs modified with CD63 apt was obtained after the first magnetic separation. As the cholesterol modified HP2 could not be captured by MNPs-CD63 apt, a large amount of activator remained in the supernatant after the second magnetic separation. Subsequently, the cholesterol modified HP2 could construct a stable ternary complex with Cas12a/crRNA to digest T40 into small poly-T ssDNA fragments. Since small poly-T ssDNA fragments were unable to form stable dsDNA with A40, Gelred was in a liberated state due to its minimal affinity with A40. The red fluorescence was off and only the green fluorescence of the g-CDs was visible under the UV laser.

Additionally, to confirm the versatility of the ratiometric biosensor, we applied the g-CDs/Gelred system to apurinic/apyrimidinic endonuclease 1 (APE1) assay, which has been confirmed to be a tumor marker [36–38]. Here, an APE1 detection diagram is presented (Scheme 1B). When the target APE1 was involved, it may disrupt the AP site modified HP2 (HP2-AP) by causing a nick at the AP site, thereby diminishing the efficacy of HP2-AP to stimulate Cas12a. Thus, T40 would be degraded to large poly-T ssDNA fragments, and a stable dsDNA structure can still be formed by adding A40. Then the Gelred would embed into dsDNA to emit red fluorescence. Following the application of g-CDs, orange fluorescence was viewed.

Overall, the g-CDs/Gelred probe could not only accurately quantify exosome or APE1 by ratiometric fluorescence, but also generated visual signal output (Scheme 1C). As the exosome or APE1 concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of Gelred at 605 nm gradually increased, while the fluorescence of g-CDs at 520 nm remained unchanged. And the fluorescence ratio of F605 to F520 (F605/F520) was improved with increasing concentrations of the target. Moreover, the fluorescence color transition of “green-yellow-orange” could be observed under the UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of Gelred in CRISPR/Cas12a system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve the optimal fluorescence efficiency of Gelred in the CRISPR/Cas12a system, we undertook a series of studies to improve experimental conditions. First, several ssDNA of 40-base-length (A40, T40, C40, T40, R40, and RC40) or dsDNA (A40/T40, C40/G40, R40/RC40) were engineered to investigate their affinity with Gelred. R40 represented a random sequence of 40 bases, and RC40 was the complementary sequence of R40. As depicted in Fig. 1 A, the binding efficiency of Gelred to different DNA sequences showed a decreasing trend: R40/RC40 > A40/T40 > (R40 ≈ RC40) > (C40/G40 ≈ T40) > (C40 ≈ G40 ≈ A40). This experimental phenomenon could be supported by previously reported studies. For example, Sapia et al. identified that Gelred had a significantly stronger affinity with T20 than A20 and C20 [39]. And Hao et al. found that Gelred's binding ability to dsDNA(A20/T20) was significantly inferior to that of (AC)10/(TG)10 [40].

Subsequently, the response of Gelred to CRISPR/Cas12a in the absence or presence of hairpin activator HP2 was investigated (Fig. 1B and Fig. 1C). For instance, “T40-A40” signified the Cas12a reacted with T40 then introduced the complementary chains A40 and Gelred. By binding with the hairpin activator HP2, Cas12a/crRNA was activated to degrade T40. Consequently, the latterly added A40 was present in the solution as single-stranded DNA. Since A40 had a limited binding affinity with Gelred, the system exhibited fluorescence-off behavior. And the T40-A40 model was adopted in subsequent research because the highest signal-to-background ratio was achieved. Furthermore, the activation efficiencies of three activators (20nt, HP1, HP2) were compared to assess the potential impact of PAM sequence to Cas12a (Fig. 1D). The correlation of activation effectiveness could be determined from the trend of fluorescence intensity in Fig. 1E: HP2 > HP1 > 20nt. At the same activator concentration, the highest signal-to-background ratio could be obtained when HP2 was selected as the activator (Fig. 1F). As a result, the PAM sequence (AAAG-TTTC) in the stem of HP2 hairpin could interact with the PAM-interacting (PI) action site on Cas12a, thus improving HP2’s ability to locate Cas12a/crRNA. Furthermore, the assembled HP2/Cas12a/crRNA had a particularly superior stability, which facilitated a more significant cleaving efficiency against T40. Despite the truth that the stem part of hairpin HP1 was a random sequence (ACTA-TGAC), the phosphate skeleton chain was still capable of forming hydrogen bonds with the PI action site on Cas12a [41]. Therefore, the activation capacity of HP1 to Cas12a was stronger than 20nt.

For further elucidating the fluorescence alterations in Fig. 1E, agarose gel electrophoresis analysis was executed on the reaction products of Cas12a with different activators. Fig. 1G illustrated the electrophoretic band of the T40-A40 system. It could be observed that a prominent band (Lane 2) emerged when the full-length ssDNA activator (20nt) was adopted. Due to the relatively low ability of 20nt to activate Cas12a, T40 is cleaved into larger poly-T ssDNA fragments by Cas12a. And the large fragments of poly-T ssDNA could still complement with A40 to form stable dsDNA. Consequently, a fluorescent band similar to that of A40/T40 dsDNA (Lane 1) was still observed in Lane 2 (Fig. 1G). Additionally, HP1 (Lane 3) had a brighter band than HP2 (Lane 4) since HP1 did not contain PAM sequence, resulting in HP1 having a diminished propensity to activate Cas12a. The Lane 4 did not display any bands, because the PAM sequence in HP2 had an improved ability to activate Cas12a. T40 was cleaved completely and could not form stable dsDNA with A40. Consequently, only intact A40 chains were present in the medium, while A40 could scarcely bind to Gelred (Fig. 1A). As well, the absence of a fluorescent band in Lane 5 indicated that the affinity between Gelred and A40 was very weak. The fluorescence intensity of the electrophoretic bands (Lanes 2–4) in Fig. 1G corresponded to that in Fig. 1E. As shown in Fig. 1H, it could be observed that T40 had a light band (Lane 1), which is because Gelred was employed as fluorescence reporter in the gelatinization process, while T40 had a weak affinity for Gelred (Fig. 1A). In addition, all the activators (20nt, HP1 and HP2) could trigger Cas12a to degrade T40 into poly-T ssDNA fragments (Lanes 2–4). Previous studies have shown that the longer the poly-T ssDNA, the stronger its affinity for Gelred [39]. Owing to the weak affinity between these poly-T ssDNA fragments and Gelred, the corresponding electrophoretic band (Lanes 2–4) could not be observed in Fig. 1H.

Subsequently, poly-T ssDNA substrate lengths were optimized (Fig. 2 A and Fig. 2B). Results indicated that the highest signal-to-background ratio was obtained in the T40-A40 system. Thus, the T40-A40 system was adopted for the following experiments. Furthermore, when 25 nM Cas12a/crRNA was reacted with T40 for 100 min, the fluorescence intensity of the T40-A40 system reached a plateau (Fig. 2C and D). Therefore, this condition was chosen as the optimal reaction condition for the T40-A40 system. As exhibited in Fig. 2E and F, with optimal operating parameters, the red fluorescence of Gelred at 605 nm gradually declined as the progressive elevation in HP2 concentration. A fluorescence plateau was achieved when HP2 concentration was 3.5 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and visualization of g-CDs/Gelred probe in CRISPR/Cas12a
ce:section.ce:para: After a series of characterization experiments (Fig. S2), the g-CDs were introduced to CRISPR/Cas12a system. Fig. 3 A illustrated the basic principle of g-CDs/Gelred probe. In the presence of activator, T40 was cleaved by triggered Cas12a and could not bind with A40 to form T40/A40 dsDNA. Only the fluorescence of g-CDs was displayed, and the red fluorescence of Gelred was turned off due to its weak affinity with ssDNA. As shown in Fig. 3B, Cas12a could not be activated to degrade T40 when the activator was absent. Hence, the intact T40 would bind to A40 to form a stable dsDNA (A40/T40). Then, Gelred was inserted into A40/T40 to produce bright red fluorescence, named T40-A40 system. Afterwards, the g-CDs were introduced to T40-A40 system, and the g-CDs/Gelred probe was therefore constructed. As the mixed color of green and red is orange, the solution produced bright orange fluorescence.

Fluorescence experiments were performed to validate the successful construction of the g-CDs/Gelred probe. Following the addition of g-CDs to the T40-A40 system, the intensity of g-CD fluorescence at 520 nm remained constant, whereas the intensity of Gelred fluorescence at 605 nm gradually weakened with the increase of HP2 concentration. Accordingly, an orange-yellow-green fluorescence color switch was observed (Fig. 3C). A plateau was obtained when HP2 concentration was 3.5 nM, assessed by the ratiometric fluorescence intensity of Gelred to g-CDs (Fig. 3D). Considering that the T40-A40 system generates a rich color transition phenomenon when g-CDs are employed, and the naked eye is quite sensitive to “orange-yellow-green” fluorescence, g-CDs are particularly effective for constructing visual ratiometric fluorescence probes. Moreover, the application of g-CDs could avoid the background interference caused by blue fluorescence in biological matrix. Afterwards, we adopted the g-CDs/Gelred probe as a general visualization platform for exosome and APE1 analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis performance of the constructed system for exosome
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve quantitative analysis of exosome, we introduced the magnetic separation technology to the g-CDs/Gelred probe. Several characterization assays were performed to confirm the successful synthesis and modification of MNPs (Fig. S3). Fig. S3E exhibited that MNP surface potential declined gradually during the modification of carboxyl group and aptamer. A DNA absorption peak was observed at 260 nm with MNPs-CD63 apt, suggesting that MNPs-COOH were efficiently modified with CD63 apt (Fig. S3F). Further, we carried out TEM, NTA, and WB analyses to demonstrate that exosomes had been successfully extracted from MCF-7 starvation culture supernatant (Fig. S4).

Moreover, to investigate whether the cholesterol modified activator will affect Cas12a activation, we extended the 3′ and 5′ termini of HP2 to obtain two kind cholesterol modified activator. According to Fig. S5, cholesterol modified at the 3′ termini had a substantially reduced activation capability. The conceivable mechanism was that the cholesterol modification at the 3′ termini of HP2 impeded the affinity of activator to Cas12a/crRNA through steric hindrance, so the 5′ termini modified HP2 was selected to anchor on the surface of exosomes.

A variety of studies were executed to assess the feasibility of this approach for exosome analysis. As pictured in Fig. 4 A and Fig. 4B, an exosome lysis buffer was transferred to the magnetically separated MNPs/Exosome/Chol-2 complex. Upon magnetic precipitation, Chol-2 in the supernatant conjugated with Gelred, leading to red fluorescence. While in the absence of Chol-2, there is no fluorescence emitted. These discoveries revealed that the cholesterol modified HP2 played an essential role in anchoring exosomes. Additionally, fluorescence and electrophoresis experiments were performed to evaluate the feasibility of g-CDs/Gelred for exosome determination (Fig. S6).

To achieve the most effective detection performance of exosomes, experimental conditions were optimized. MNPs-CD63 apt was best concentrated at 0.25 mg/mL, the optimal reaction time of MNPs-CD63 apt with exosomes was 60 min, and the efficient reaction time of exosomes with Chol-2 was 60 min (Fig. S7). Under optimized factors, the performance of exosome analysis was investigated by using the g-CDs/Gelred probe. Fig. 4C illustrated that with elevated exosome concentrations, the fluorescence intensity at 605 nm gradually became higher. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of g-CDs at 520 nm maintained, causing an overall color shift of “green-yellow-orange” under ultraviolet light. The exosome concentration was linearly fitted with the signal response of F605/F520. It was determined that F = 3.624 × 10-8 c + 0.05766, giving a linear range of 7.5 × 106–4.5 × 107 particles/mL (Fig. 4D). According to 3σ/K rule, the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.4 × 105 particles/mL.

Furthermore, we investigated the selectivity of the biosensor to exosome. Exosomes were replaced by enzymes that interact with DNA or interferon that may be present in biological specimens (Fig. S8A). As a result, only exosome triggered obvious responses in the system, validating robust selectivity. Further, it was observed that the system had no significant change in fluorescence intensity after mixing interferon with exosome, confirming its exceptional anti-interference effectiveness (Fig. S8B). We also observed that the probe was very stable when detecting exosome through repeatability tests (Fig. S9).

Finally, 9 μL of MCF-7 cell starvation supernatant was utilized to quantify exosome by mixing it with low, medium, and high concentration gradient exosome extracts. The result showed that the abundance of exosome in MCF-7 cell starvation culture supernatant was 1.002 × 107 particles/mL. The recoveries were 90 %-110 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 5 %, indicating that the sensor is quite accurate and precise (Table 1 ). As shown in Fig. S10, the response of g-CDs/Gelred induced by exosome derived from MCF-10A cells was significantly weaker than that of MCF-7, providing evidence that CD63 protein expression on exosome derived from normal cells was lower than that of malignant cells. By comparing with the other exosome sensing strategies, our research had a lower LOD, thus demonstrating the superior sensitivity of the g-CDs/Gelred probe (Table S2). In conclusion, this method took only one hour to capture exosomes in trace samples, and the exosomes could be analyzed simultaneously by ratiometric fluorescence and visualization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis performance of the constructed system for APE1
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the feasibility of g-CDs/Gelred probe for APE1 analysis, corresponding fluorescence and electrophoresis experiments were undertaken (Fig. S11). During the optimization experiment, the reaction time of 60 min was confirmed to be satisfactory for APE1 and HP2-AP (Fig. S12). Under the favorable parameters, the performance of the platform was explored with different gradient concentrations of APE1. According to Fig. 5 A, with the increase of APE1 concentration, the g-CDs/Gelred system had a constant fluorescence intensity at 520 nm, whereas the fluorescence at 605 nm gradually improved. Upon the increase of APE1 concentration, the fluorescence color change of “green-yellow-orange” was visible by the naked eye. Fig. 5B presented the linear diagram of APE1, it was calculated that F = 6.719c + 0.0551 (r = 0.9982). The linear range was 0.05–0.25 U/mL, with a limit of detection 0.0012 U/mL (3σ/K). The LOD of our sensor is much smaller than other analysis approaches, which proves the g-CDs/Gelred probe has superior sensitivity for APE1 (Table S3).

Afterwards, we evaluated the selectivity and anti-interference performance of the sensor in determining APE1 (Fig. S13). And we also conducted repeatability experiments of the g-CDs/Gelred probe for APE1 assay (Fig. S14). Additionally, the biosensor was applied to the determination of APE1 in MCF-7 cell lysates. Based on the experimental results, the probe revealed a significant linear correlation between 350 and 2200 Cells/mL, and naked eye visual differentiation was realized under UV light (Fig. 6 A and Fig. 6B). The fluorescence signals were also obtained from the same number (5000 Cells/mL) of different cancer cell lysates (Fig. 6C).

To develop a visual ratiometric fluorescence probe and adapt it as a universal platform, we investigated the affinity of Gelred with DNA of diverse sequences, the signal-to-background ratio after CRISPR/Cas12a cleaved different DNA, the activation ability of three activators to Cas12a, and the visualization capability through utilizing CDs. In addition, the g-CDs/Gelred probe was effectively employed to the determination of exosome and APE1 in actual specimens. And exosome could be quantified with only 9 μL actual samples due to the enrichment of magnetic beads and the signal amplification of CRISPR/Cas12a. The platform devised possesses several notable benefits: there is no necessity for exorbitant labeling and elaborate purification of DNA, and the substrate chain employed was obtained by HAP purification technique instead of the more expensive HPLC purification; CDs are introduced as a built-in correction to fabricate a ratiometric probe, allowing it to resist fluctuations during measurement, thus enhancing the detection accuracy; and the excellent degree of signal recovery and low background signal enable the probe to achieve a high signal-to-background ratio, facilitating the analysis of target concentrations over a broad range; additionally, the significant enhancement of Cas12a activation by the extension of the PAM sequence in the activator, in conjunction with the inherent high-efficiency collateral cleavage activity of Cas12a, enables the g-CDs/Gelred probe to demonstrate superior sensitivity; moreover, g-CDs/Gelred offers a visual “green-yellow-orange” mode under UV lamp; as a final characteristic, the biosensor has the capability of detecting multiple objects by adjusting the activator, thus making it a versatile platform. Nevertheless, this strategy involves certain limitations as well. For example, Cas12a needs a longer splicing time when utilizing T40 as the substrate, since ssDNA with 40 bases is tricky to cleave into small fragments compared with F-Q probes comprised only 7 bases. Our forthcoming research will strive at remedying this flaw. In addition, due to the exceptional coupling properties of the CRISPR/Cas12a system to functional nucleic acids, the system is expected to be combined with diverse DNAzymes, G-quadruplexes, and aptamers to widen the potential applications. Meanwhile, we intend to integrate the g-CDs/Gelred probe with signal amplification techniques such as catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA), hybrid chain reaction (HCR), rolling ring amplification (RCA) to obtain superior sensitivity.

Zhigang Liang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiang Chen: Validation, Software. Yan Yu: Validation, Conceptualization. Guanhong Xu: Software, Data curation. Fangdi Wei: Software, Investigation. Jing Yang: Supervision, Software. Qin Hu: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yao Cen: Supervision, Software, Conceptualization.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81973283, 61775099, 21705080), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Nos. BK20221304, BK20171487, BK20171043), “Blue Project” Foundation of the Higher Education Institutions of Jiangsu Province.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114010.

ce:section.ce:para:
